title: A Newly Discovered Recording Lets You Hear Delta Blues Legend Robert Johnson in Stunning Clarity
description:
in History, Music |   	April 17th, 2026  3 Comments
	
			

	

Great swathes of rock music since the nineยญteen-sixยญties would nevยญer have existยญed, w
body:
in History, Music |   	April 17th, 2026  3 Comments
	
			

	

Great swathes of rock music since the nineยญteen-sixยญties would nevยญer have existยญed, weโre someยญtimes told, were it not for the recordยญings of Robert Johnยญson. Cerยญtainยญly the likes of Keiยญth Richards, Eric Clapยญton, Robert Plant, and Bob Dylan have nevยญer hesยญiยญtatยญed to acknowlยญedge his influยญence. โFrom the first note the vibraยญtions from the loudยญspeakยญer made my hair stand up,โ Dylan writes in his autoยญbiยญogยญraยญphy of his first encounter with Johnยญsonโs music. โThe stabยญbing sounds from the guiยญtar could almost break a winยญdow. When Johnยญson startยญed singing, he seemed like a guy who could have sprung from the head of Zeus in full armor. I immeยญdiยญateยญly difยญferยญenยญtiยญatยญed between him and anyยญone else I had ever heard.โ Not bad for a recordยญing oldยญer than Dylan himยญself.
In the earยญly nineยญteen-sixยญties, the blues as Johnยญson played it seems to have soundยญed elecยญtriยญfyยญingยญly revยญeยญlaยญtoยญry to the genยญerยญaยญtion of then-young musiยญcians who manยญaged to hear it, regardยญless of their own oriยญgins. All such recordยญings date from 1936 or 1937, the fruits of just two sesยญsionsย in makeshift Texas stuยญdios overยญseen by proยญducยญer Don Law.
Though the โking of the Delta blues singersโ left behind only this small body of work after his still-unexยญplained death at the age of 27, itโs been endยญlessยญly scruยญtiยญnized by the genยญreโs enthuยญsiยญasts. All of them will sureยญly regard as a godยญsend the newยญly disยญcovยญered shelยญlac masยญter test pressยญing above of โCross Road Blues,โ a song that plays an outยญsized part in the legยญend of Robert Johnยญson, who some say sold his soul to the devยญil at just such a locaยญtion in exchange for his forยญmiยญdaยญble guiยญtar skills.
Though it conยญtains no refยญerยญence to any such unholy pact, nor to any denizen of the underยญworld, โCross Road Bluesโ does have a hauntยญing sound that goes with the shadยญowy ambiยญence of the manโs short life stoยญry. Some of that had to do with the less-than-ideยญal qualยญiยญty of the recordยญings that have long cirยญcuยญlatยญed, but this test pressยญing of Johnยญsonโs secยญond take sounds difยญferยญent. Uploaded by sound restorยญer Nick Delยญlow, it was origยญiยญnalยญly made in 1940 straight from the metยญal masยญter by Columยญbia Records proยญducยญer George Avakian, who would go on to work with everyยญone from Miles Davis to Edith Piaf to John Cage. The sonยญic mudยญdiยญness of most Robert Johnยญson releasยญes thus far has done its part to preยญvent modยญern-day lisยญtenยญers from getยญting quite what the big deal was about him. But perยญhaps the unpreceยญdentยญed clarยญiยญty of this recordยญing will get the hairย of young musiยญcians and mature conยญnoisยญseurs alike standยญing on end.
via Ted Gioia
Relatยญed conยญtent:
Keiยญth Richards Shows Us How to Play the Blues, Inspired by Robert Johnยญson, on the Acoustic Guiยญtar
Covยญerยญing Robert Johnsonโs Blues Became a Rite of Rock โnโ Roll Pasยญsage: Hear Covยญers by The Rolling Stones, Eric Clapยญton, Howlยญinโ Wolf, Lucinยญda Williams & More
Robert Johnยญson Finalยญly Gets an Obitยญuยญary in The New York Times 81 Years After His Death
Jimi Henยญdrix Plays the Delta Blues on a 12-String Acoustic Guiยญtar in 1968, and Jams with His Blues Idols, Budยญdy Guy & B.B. King
The Legยญend of How Bluesยญman Robert Johnยญson Sold His Soul to the Devยญil at the Crossยญroads
A Brief Hisยญtoยญry of Makยญing Deals with the Devยญil: Nicยญcolรฒ Paganiยญni, Robert Johnยญson, Jimยญmy Page & More
Based in Seoul,ย Colยญinย Marshallย writes and broadยญcasts on cities, lanยญguage, and culยญture. Heโs the author of the newsletยญterย Books on Citiesย as well as the booksย ํ๊ตญ ์์ฝ ๊ธ์งย (No Sumยญmaยญrizยญing Korea) andย Koreยญan Newtro.ย Folยญlow him on the social netยญwork forยญmerยญly known as Twitยญter atย @colinmarshall.

title: How George Orwell Predicted the Rise of โAI Slopโ in Nineteen Eighty-Four (1949)
description:
in Books, Literature, Technology |   	April 16th, 2026  4 Comments
	
			
Weโve lived but a few years so far into the age when artiยญfiยญcial intelยญliยญ
body:
in Books, Literature, Technology |   	April 16th, 2026  4 Comments
	
			
Weโve lived but a few years so far into the age when artiยญfiยญcial intelยญliยญgence can proยญduce conยญvincยญing stoยญries, songs, essays, poems, novยญels, and even films. For many of us, these recentยญly impleยญmentยญed funcยญtions have already come to feel necยญesยญsary in our daiยญly life, but it may surยญprise us to conยญsidยญer how many peoยญple had long assumed that comยญputยญers could already perยญform them. That belief sureยญly owes in part to the roles played by effecยญtiveยญly senยญtient machines in popยญuยญlar ficยญtions since at least the earยญly decades of the twenยญtiยญeth cenยญtuยญry. Revisยญitยญing George Orwellโs Nineยญteen Eighty-Four, we even find a device very much like todayโs large lanยญguage modยญels in use at the Minยญistry of Truth, the employยญer of proยญtagยญoยญnist Winยญston Smith.
Withยญin the Minยญistry is โa whole chain of sepยญaยญrate departยญments dealยญing with proยญleยญtarยญiยญan litยญerยญaยญture, music, draยญma, and enterยญtainยญment genยญerยญalยญly. Here were proยญduced rubยญbishy newsยญpaยญpers conยญtainยญing almost nothยญing except sport, crime and astrolยญoยญgy, senยญsaยญtionยญal five-cent novยญelยญettes, films oozยญing with sex, and senยญtiยญmenยญtal songs which were comยญposed entireยญly by mechanยญiยญcal means on a speยญcial kind of kaleiยญdoยญscope known as a verยญsiยญfiยญcaยญtor.โ Much latยญer in the novยญel, Smith overยญhears a hit song comยญposed on that very kaleiยญdoยญscope, โwithยญout any human interยญvenยญtion whatยญevยญer,โ sung by a woman of this dystopiยญan Engยญlandโs lowยญest class, whose very baseยญness libยญerยญates it from the watchยญful eye that Big Brothยญerโs vast surยญveilยญlance sysยญtem keeps on his ostenยญsiยญbly privยญiยญleged Parยญty memยญbers.
All the โproยญlesโ realยญly require, in the view of the state, is the freeยญdom to satยญisยญfy their vices and a steady stream of paciยญfyยญing media. The extruยญsions of the verยญsiยญfiยญcaยญtor may now bring to mind the ever-increasยญing quanยญtiยญties of โAI slop,โ often creยญatยญed with vanยญishยญingยญly small amounts of human interยญvenยญtion, whose potenยญtial to flood the interยญnet has lateยญly become a matยญter of pubยญlic conยญcern. Whatโs more chillยญing to conยญsidยญer is that such low-effort, high-volยญume conยญtent wouldยญnโt have attained such a presยญence if it werenโt genยญuineยญly popยญuยญlar. Much like the junk culยญture pumped out by the Minยญistry of Truth, AI slop reflects less the ill intent of (or at least neglect by) the powยญers that be than the undeยญmandยญing nature of the pubยญlic.
Perยญhaps we can proยญviยญsionยญalยญly chalk this one up in the โOrwell was rightโ colยญumn. Itโs posยญsiยญble that, in light of real techยญnoยญlogยญiยญcal develยญopยญments, even Isaac Asiยญmov could be conยญvinced to give it to him. Here on Open Culยญture, we recentยญly feaยญtured Asiยญmovโs criยญtique of Nineยญteen Eighty-Four as a poor propheยญcy of the future, not least from a techยญnoยญlogยญiยญcal standยญpoint. That piece was writยญten in 1980 at the very end of an โAI winยญter,โ one of the falยญlow periยญods in artiยญfiยญcial intelยญliยญgence research. A boom was soon to come, but the truยญly astonยญishยญing develยญopยญments wouldยญnโt hapยญpen until the twenยญty-twenยญties, about thirยญty years after Asiยญmovโs death. When describยญing the verยญsiยญfiยญcaยญtor, Orwell was preยญsumยญably extrapยญoยญlatยญing from the disยญtractยญing, disยญposยญable enterยญtainยญments of nineยญteen-forยญties Engยญland. Even if his readยญers couldยญnโt believe the idea of that sort of thing being creยญatยญed autoยญmatยญiยญcalยญly, more than a few probยญaยญbly agreed with his diagยญnoยญsis of its qualยญiยญty. Now, colยญlecยญtive human intelยญliยญgence may face its most forยญmiยญdaยญble chalยญlenger, but indiยญvidยญual human disยญcernยญment has nevยญer been more valuยญable.
via Boing Boing
Relatยญed conยญtent:
Isaac Asiยญmov Reviews George Orwellโs Nineยญteen Eighty-Four and Calls It โNot Sciยญence Ficยญtion, But a Disยญtortยญed Nosยญtalยญgia for a Past that Nevยญer Wasโ
George Orwell Preยญdictยญed Camยญeras Would Watch Us in Our Homes; He Nevยญer Imagยญined Weโd Gladยญly Buy and Install Them Ourยญselves
Aldous Huxยญley to George Orwell: My Hellยญish Vision of the Future is Betยญter Than Yours (1949)
Sci-Fi Writer Arthur C. Clarke Preยญdicts the Future in 1964: Artiยญfiยญcial Intelยญliยญgence, Instanยญtaยญneous Globยญal Comยญmuยญniยญcaยญtion, Remote Work, Sinยญguยญlarยญiยญty & More
Based in Seoul,ย Colยญinย Marshallย writes and broadยญcasts on cities, lanยญguage, and culยญture. Heโs the author of the newsletยญterย Books on Citiesย as well as the booksย ํ๊ตญ ์์ฝ ๊ธ์งย (No Sumยญmaยญrizยญing Korea) andย Koreยญan Newtro.ย Folยญlow him on the social netยญwork forยญmerยญly known as Twitยญter atย @colinmarshall.

title: Watch La Linea, the Popular 1970s Italian Animations Drawn with a Single Line
description:
in Animation |   	April 16th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	


Simยญplicยญiยญty is not the goal. It is the by-prodยญuct of a good idea and modยญest expec
body:
in Animation |   	April 16th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	


Simยญplicยญiยญty is not the goal. It is the by-prodยญuct of a good idea and modยญest expecยญtaยญtions.
Thus spake designยญer Paul Rand, a man who knew someยญthing about makยญing an impresยญsion, havยญing creยญatยญed iconยญic logos for such immeยญdiยญateยญly recยญogยญnizยญable brands as ABC, IBM, and UPS.
An examยญple of Randโs obserยญvaยญtion, La Linยญea, aka Mr. Line, a beloved and decepยญtiveยญly simยญple carยญtoon charยญacยญter drawn with a sinยญgle unbroยญken line, began as a shill for an Italยญian cookยญware comยญpaยญny. No matยญter what he manยญages to get up to in two or three minยญutes, itโs deterยญmined that heโll evenยญtuยญalยญly butt up against the limยญiยญtaยญtions of his linยญeal realยญiยญty.
His chatยญterยญing, apoplecยญtic response proved such a hit with viewยญers that a few episodes in, the cookยญware conยญnecยญtion was sevยญered. Mr. Line went on to become a globยญal star in his own right, appearยญing in 90ย short aniยญmaยญtions throughยญout his 15-year hisยญtoยญry, startยญing in 1971. Find many of the episodes on YouTube here.
The forยญmuยญla does sound rather simยญple. Aniยญmaยญtor Osvalยญdo Cavanยญdoli starts each episode by drawยญing a horยญiยญzonยญtal line in white grease penยญcil. The line takes on human form. Mr. Lineโs a zesty guy, the sort who throws himยญself into whatยญevยญer it is heโs doing, whether ogling girls at the beach, playยญing clasยญsiยญcal piano or ice skatยญing.
Whenยญevยญer he bumps up against an obstacleโan uncrossยญable gap in his baseยญline, an inadยญverยญtentยญly explodยญed penisโhe calls upon the godยญlike hand of the aniยญmaยญtor to make things right.
(Bawdy humor is a staยญple of La Linยญea, though the visuยญal forยญmat keeps things fairยญly chaste. Innuยญenยญdo aside, itโs about as graphยญic as aย big rigโsย silยญhouยญetยญted mudยญflap girl.)
Voiceover artist Carยญlo Bonoยญmi conยญtributes a large part of the charm. Mr. Line may speak with an Italยญian accent, but his vocal track is 90% improยญvised gibยญberยญish, with a smatยญterยญing of Lomยญbard dialect. Watch him chanยญnel the charยญacยญter in the recordยญing booth, below.
I love hearยญingย him take the even-keeled Cavanยญdoli to task. I donโt speak Italยญian, but I had the senยญsaยญtion I underยญstood where both playยญers are comยญing from in the scene below.


	

Watch a big marathon of La Linยญea at the top, or the comยญplete colยญlecยญtion here.
Note: An earยญliยญer verยญsion of this post appeared on our site in 2015.
Relatยญed Conยญtent:
The Disยญney Carยญtoon That Introยญduced Mickยญey Mouse & Aniยญmaยญtion with Sound (1928)
Conยญfiยญdence: The Carยญtoon That Helped Amerยญiยญca Get Through the Great Depresยญsion (1933)
Watch Aniยญmaยญtions of Two Itaยญlo Calviยญno Stoยญries: โThe False Grandยญmothยญerโ and โThe Disยญtance from the Moonโ
Ayun Halยญlยญiยญday is an author and illusยญtraยญtor in NYC.

title: Anarchic Cats Are Ensnared in Chaos in Lรฉo Forest's Dynamic Drawings
description: Feline antics are notoriously chaotic. โThe cat is, above all things, a dramatist,โ author and Egyptologist Margaret Benson is to have said. Sacre
body:
Feline antics are notoriously chaotic. โThe cat is, above all things, a dramatist,โ author and Egyptologist Margaret Benson is to have said. Sacred to ancient Egyptians, domestic cats share more than 95% of their genetic makeup with tigers, and they can leap five times their height and turn into veritable spring mechanisms when startled. Also, would the Internet be the same without cat memes? For Lรฉo Forest, these lovable, independent, wily, and territorial creatures provide an endless source of inspiration for dynamic pencil drawings.
The Paris-based artistโs playful works tap into the physical and emotional quirks of cats, from brawling pairs to individuals in the midst of grooming, scratching, or attacking. Flailing limbs and blurred motion evoke Italian Futurist painter Giacomo Ballaโs seminal painting, โDynamism of a Dog on a Leashโ (1912) in which a Dachsund and its ownerโs feet are fuzzily multiplied to imply very quick movement.

Forest is currently working toward a project with Moosey in London, where prints are available. Follow him on Instagram for updates.

schema.headline: Anarchic Cats Are Ensnared in Chaos in Lรฉo Forest's Dynamic Drawings

title: Marvel at Manabu Kosaka's Hyperrealistic Paper Sculptures of Retro Objects
description: Itโs one thing to marvel at the inner workings of a transistor radio or a timepiece, but for artist Manabu Kosaka, that curiosity reaches a whole
body:
Itโs one thing to marvel at the inner workings of a transistor radio or a timepiece, but for artist Manabu Kosaka, that curiosity reaches a whole new level. Using nothing but paper, the artist makes scale replicas of cameras, watches, gaming consoles, shoes, food, and more with a preternatural attention to detail. Not only does a 35mm film camera include a strap and a back hatch that opens, the lever used to advance the film and other gears are also built into the top, some of which are even moveable.
Around ten years ago, Kosaka faced uncertainty about the direction of his work. โDuring that time, I spoke with a friend who works in art direction, and they suggested that I try creating with simpler materials in a more minimal way,โ he tells Colossal. โThat advice stayed with me, and gradually I began focusing on paper as my primary material, eventually deciding to work exclusively with it.โ
35mm camera
Through a meticulous process of cutting, folding, and scoring, Kosaka creates incredibly realistic depictions of everyday objects, often with a retro twist. He carefully studies the mechanics of the real objects, disassembling them in order to replicate individual components inside. He is currently working on a model of a Playstation 2 console, which was originally released in 2000. 
โWhat I love most about paper is its incredible flexibility,โ Kosaka says. โIt responds to my ideas almost completelyโbeyond what I expect, even. It allows me to express what I want in a very direct way, while also feeling that it can become almost anything.โ
See much more on the artistโs Instagram.

title: A Giant Wool Form by Nicola Turner Heaves and Skitters Through an 18th-Century Chapel
description: In a converted 18th-century chapel on the grounds of Yorkshire Sculpture Park, a strange form creeps through openings in the architecture. One can
body:
In a converted 18th-century chapel on the grounds of Yorkshire Sculpture Park, a strange form creeps through openings in the architecture. One can imagine its clipper- and knife-footed tendrils scurrying across the floor as it spills from an upper aperture and even slithers around part of the buildingโs exterior. Its otherworldly genesis is at the hands of Nicola Turner, known for her monumental, contorted textile installations that often heave and surge from structures and public spaces.
Turnerโs solo exhibition, Timeโs Scythe, comprises forms made of recycled wool and horsehair, which she hand-stitches inside of mesh to create the bulging, knotted forms. โThis is Turnerโs first large-scale installation to use pale wool and creates a different energy to her dark sculptures, moving away from their more melancholic character,โ the gallery says.

Timeโs Scythe continues through September 27 in Wakefield. If you go, check out LR Vandyโs provocative exhibition, Rise, which also continues into September. See more on Turnerโs Instagram, and for more twisting, creature-like forms, might also enjoy the work of Kate MccGwire.

schema.headline: A Giant Wool Form by Nicola Turner Heaves and Skitters Through an 18th-Century Chapel

title: 15 Artists Explore the Potentiality of Fabric and Fiber in 'Textile Art Redefined'
description: Until just the past few decades, textiles were generally created with only practical applications in mind. Although fiber and cloth in its myriad
body:
Until just the past few decades, textiles were generally created with only practical applications in mind. Although fiber and cloth in its myriad forms had been produced for millennia around the globe, fabrics were woven for either domestic or industrial use, and crafts such as knitting, weaving, basket- and net-making, and more were considered purely functional. Think clothing or decor. Even ornate medieval tapestries were conceived as utilitarian objects, used in stone buildings like churches and large homes to soften sounds and insulate against the cold.
Within the canon of Western art history, in particular, the hierarchy of fine art has long been quite definite: painting and sculpture were chief among mediums. Anything else fell under categories of preparatory processes, craft, or ornament. But in the mid-20th century, that delineation began to shift. Anni Albers, who taught at the Bauhaus and later Black Mountain College, was one of the first artists to approach weaving as both a craft and an art. She laid the foundations for later artists like Sheila Hicks, Magdalena Abakanowicz, Cecelia Vicuรฑa, Faith Ringgold, and many more. Today, artists like Bisa Butler, Do Ho Suh, Nick Cave, and countless others continue to challenge boundaries and redefine the role of textiles in art.
Chiachio & Giannone. โConversaciรณn sobre arteโ (2022), hand-embroidered quilt with cotton threads on toile de Jouy, 164 x 200 centimeters. Photo by Nacho Iasparra. Courtesy of Bendana | Pinel Art Contemporain, Paris
A new exhibition at Saatchi Gallery takes a fresh look at how fiber has become a celebrated facet of contemporary art. Textile Art Redefined is curated by Helen Adams, founder of the platform Textile Curator. Vibrant colors and patterns infuse the show with a sense of joy and optimism, while the selection highlights the broad range of approaches to different materials, such as Ian Berryโs large-scale installation titled โSecret Garden,โ which mimics Delft tiles and yet is made entirely of recycled denim. And Kenny Nguyenโs large-scale wall piece, made of thousands of colorful strips of silk, appears to undulate and swirl.
For some of these artists, like Argentinian duo Chiachio & Giannone and knit designer Kaffe Fassettโwho continues to work alongside his long-term partner, designer Brandon Mablyโthe union of art and craft lends itself to a entire lifestyle built around fiber as a form of expression, brimming with color and patterns. Colossal readers may also recognize work by Anne von Freyburg, Signe Emdal, Deniz Kurdak, and Benjamin Shine, in addition to Caroline Burgess, Sara Impey, Simone Pheulpin, Jakkai Siributr, Magda Sayeg, and Jenni Dutton. โIn an increasingly digital world, creating by hand has taken on a new appreciation,โ says a statement.
Textile Art Redefined continues through May 10 in London. Adams is also the author of the new book Textile Fine Art, published by Laurence King.
Anne von Freyburg, โIn Flight Mode (After Fragonard, The Swing)โ (2026), textile wall installation painting: acrylic ink, synthetic fabrics, PVC fabric, tapestry-fabric, sequin fabrics, hand-embroidery, polyester wadding and hand-dyed tassel fringes on canvas, 350 x 260 centimeters. Courtesy of K Contemporary Denver + Santa Fe. Photo by Pasquale Viglione
Installation view of โTextile Art Redefined,โ Saatchi Gallery, London. Photo by Pasquale Viglione
Ian Berry, โThe Secret Gardenโ (2026), denim on denim, dimensions variable. Photo by Debbie Bragg
Ian Berry, โThe Secret Gardenโ (detail). Photo by Debbie Bragg
